data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
07:30 AM Fed's Williams Speech

New York Federal Reserve Bank President John Williams gives keynote before the Annual Conference of the Central Bank of Chile.

08:30 AM Fed's Barr Speech

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr will give welcome remarks at the College Fed Challenge Finals, Washington, D.C.

09:00 AM Fed's Logan Speech

Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan participates in moderated panel before the"SNB and its Watchers 2025" event.

09:00 AM Fed's Collins Speech 

Boston Federal Reserve Bank President Susan Collins is scheduled to deliver opening remarks and appear in a televised interview at the Federal Reserve Bank of Boston. 

09:45 AM PMI Composite Flash

The flash Composite Purchasing Managers' Index provides an early estimate of current private sector output by combining information obtained from surveys of around 1,000 manufacturing and service sector companies.

The manufacturing PMI is expected at 52.3 for the November flash versus 52.5 in the October final. Services is seen at 54.8 versus 54.8 in the October final. 10:00 AM Consumer Sentiment 

The University of Michigan's Consumer Survey Center questions households each month on their assessment of current conditions and expectations of future conditions.

The index is expected to be at 50.5 in the November final, from 50.3 in the prior.

10:00 AM Wholesale Inventories (Preliminary)

Wholesale trade measures the dollar value of sales made and inventories held by merchant wholesalers. It is a component of business sales and inventories.

Forecasters expect inventories to decrease by 0.2% m-o-m.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

data.title: MINISO Group Non-GAAP EPADS of $0.35 beats by $0.02, revenue of $814.3M beats by $23.27M
data.description:
• MINISO Group Holding press release (MNSO): Q3 Non-GAAP EPADS of $0.35 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $814.3M (+28.2% Y/Y) beats by $23.27M.
  • Adjusted EBITDA(3) increased 18.8% year over year to RMB1,353.8 million (US$190.2 million).
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin(3) was 23.4%, compared to 25.2% in the same period last year.
  • Total number of stores on group level was 8,138 as of September 30, 2025, achieving the milestone of 8,000 stores, with a year-over-year increase of 718 net new stores.
  • Number of MINISO stores was 7,831 as of September 30, 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 645 net new stores.
    • Number of MINISO stores in mainland China was 4,407 as of September 30, 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 157 net new stores.
    • Number of MINISO stores in overseas markets reached 3,424 as of September 30, 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 488 net new stores.
  • Number of TOP TOY stores was 307 as of September 30, 2025, representing a year-over-year increase of 73 net new stores.

data.title: Copart signals robust auction returns and expanding global buyer participation while optimizing cycle times
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Copart, Inc. (CPRT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Jeffrey Liaw opened by noting "our global insurance units for the first quarter of 2026 declined 8.4% or a 5.6% decline, excluding catastrophic volumes from a year ago. Our U.S. insurance units declined 9.5% for the same period and 7.3%, excluding catastrophic activity as well" and emphasized the continued upward trend in total loss frequency, which reached 22.6% for the calendar year 2025 through September.
  • Liaw highlighted, "we are achieving all-time high average selling prices for our U.S. insurance carriers. And in fact, for the quarter, our global insurance ASPs increased 6.8%. Our U.S. insurance ASPs increased 8.4%." He attributed this to strong auction liquidity and international buyer participation.
  • Regarding the noninsurance/wholesale business, Liaw described the company’s investments in systems tailored for commercial sellers and reported that "international buyers have purchased vehicles that are 38% higher in value than comparable U.S. buyers by comparison."
  • Liaw outlined five indicators of market health: higher pure sale rates, expanding international demand, greater bidder participation, stronger pre-auction engagement, and rising gross returns, stating these are "the best long-term indicators of the strength of our business."
  • CFO Leah Stearns stated, "For the first quarter, consolidated revenue grew just under 1% year-over-year or 2.9% excluding cat, to $1.16 billion, with service revenue increasing just under 1% and purchased vehicle sales increasing nearly 2%. Our fee revenue per unit increased over 7% during the quarter, which was primarily driven by growth in our average selling prices, which have increased 8.5% from the prior year period."
  • Stearns added, "Global gross profit increased 4.9% or 3.7%, excluding cat, to $537 million. Gross profit per fee unit increased 12.3% and purchase unit gross profit decreased 3% to $22 million from the prior year period. Gross margin improved 184 basis points to 46.5%, reflecting the nonrecurrence of onetime expenses related to our cat response."

Outlook

  • Management did not provide explicit forward guidance for revenues or EPS. Liaw and Stearns reiterated their focus on sustaining auction returns and further improving operational efficiency. There was no direct update or change from prior guidance language.

Financial Results

  • Total global units sold decreased 6.7% with fee units decreasing 6.3%, influenced by the nonrecurrence of prior-year catastrophic events. Normalized for these events, global units sold decreased 4.6%.
  • Global insurance units declined 8.1% or 5.6% adjusted for cat, while global noninsurance units declined 1.5%.
  • Consolidated revenue was $1.16 billion for the quarter and global gross profit increased to $537 million. Gross margin reached 46.5%.
  • Earnings per diluted share increased 10.8% to $0.41, and net income was $404 million, up 11.5% versus last year.
  • U.S. segment highlights include a 5.3% increase in dealer unit sales and a 10.9% increase in purchased vehicle sales, with average sale prices for these units up over 50%.
  • Cycle times in the U.S. decreased by 9%, and U.S. inventory declined by just over 17% from the year-ago period.
  • Copart ended the quarter with liquidity of approximately $6.5 billion, including $5.2 billion in cash and cash equivalents and no debt.

Q&A

  • Bob Labick, CJS Securities, Inc.: Asked about the decline in unit volumes and whether any market share shifts were occurring. Liaw replied, "I think it is the factors you just described, which is principally that insurance coverage itself has changed...I don't think it's farfetched to extrapolate from that to the kind of unit trends that we're seeing in our business."
  • Labick followed up on total loss frequency trends. Liaw noted, "total loss frequency in -- as recently as 1990 was 5%. 1980, it was 4%. And today, it's 22% and change. So it's up 80 basis points versus a year ago...I think reading a whole lot into 80 basis points versus 130 or versus plus 30, I think, is more noise than it is signal."
  • Craig Kennison, Baird: Inquired about accident claim trends and the impact of ADAS technology. Liaw responded, "safety technologies very much have moved the needle and have done so for 40 years...It's been more than offset by total loss frequency."
  • Kennison asked about vehicles not covered by insurance. Liaw explained that Copart's Cash For Cars business does capture some of these vehicles but less efficiently than through insurance relationships.
  • Christopher Bottiglieri, BNP Paribas: Asked about the 38% value disparity between international and U.S. bidders. Stearns clarified, "the ASP of the vehicles that they purchase is 38% higher than the average ASP of buyers from the U.S...more focused on those borderline total losses and repairable vehicles."
  • Bret Jordan, Jefferies: Queried about market share with Progressive. Liaw stated, "the insurance industry itself has proven, over the long haul, very dynamic with different players gaining and losing share episodically over many years...the overwhelming focus is on delivering excellent gross and net returns."
  • Jeffrey Lick, Stephens: Asked about factors influencing future volumes. Liaw noted, "a soft market means that the economics of total loss, all else equal, are less costly to the insurance carriers...parts prices and repair costs...that's been the million-dollar question of this era."
  • John Healy, Northcoast Research: Sought clarity on insurance premiums and capital deployment. Stearns said, "Copart will continue to focus on deploying capital when we see areas that we believe will create meaningful long-term value for the business and for our shareholders...we've historically used the share repurchase program...That would be our lever to return capital to shareholders."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed slightly negative to neutral sentiment, pressing on volume declines, market share dynamics, and efficiency of capturing uninsured vehicles. Their tone was inquisitive and at times skeptical, seeking clarity on drivers of trends and future positioning.
  • Management’s tone during prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing competitive advantages, auction liquidity, and long-term strategic positioning. During the Q&A, management became more defensive and detailed, particularly when explaining cyclical phenomena and lack of forward guidance. Liaw frequently referenced long-term trends to deflect short-term concerns.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the analyst tone shifted from congratulatory to more probing, while management maintained confidence but addressed more immediate cyclical headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remains focused on long-term auction liquidity and operational efficiency, with no new explicit forward guidance.
  • The strategic focus continues to be expanding auction liquidity, growing international participation, and improving technology for commercial sellers.
  • Key metrics shifted: global insurance volumes moved from 4.5% growth for FY 2025 to an 8.4% decline in Q1 2026. Average selling prices saw greater increases than in the previous quarter.
  • Analysts in the current quarter were more focused on understanding volume declines, market share shifts, and the efficiency of non-insurance sourcing, compared to prior quarters’ emphasis on AI and technology.
  • Management’s tone consistently highlighted competitive strengths and investment discipline, but faced more direct questioning about short-term performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing declines in insurance unit volumes due to consumer retrenchment and changes in insurance coverage.
  • The company faces external volatility in tariffs, parts prices, and broader macroeconomic and geopolitical factors impacting equipment sales and repair costs.
  • Analysts raised concerns around market share shifts, potential changes in Progressive’s share, and the ability to capture uninsured vehicle supply efficiently.
  • Management addressed these risks by emphasizing long-term investments in auction liquidity, technology, and disciplined capital deployment.

Final Takeaway

Copart leadership emphasized that their ability to achieve record high auction returns, expand international buyer participation, and reduce cycle times underpins their long-term confidence despite current declines in insurance unit volumes. The company remains committed to disciplined investment, operational efficiency, and leveraging its robust balance sheet to drive shareholder value, positioning itself to capitalize on cyclical recovery and ongoing shifts in the global automotive marketplace.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elastic raises FY26 revenue outlook to $1.721B as AI-driven large deals accelerate
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Elastic N.V. (ESTC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Ashutosh Kulkarni, CEO, opened the call stating Q2 was an "outstanding quarter for Elastic, driven by robust growth across the company with AI positively impacting all areas of our business." He highlighted that Elastic delivered revenue growth of 16% and a non-GAAP operating margin of 16.5%. The company achieved sales-led subscription revenue growth of 18% and increased customers spending over $100,000 to more than 1,600.
  • Kulkarni described major wins, including "over 30 commitments valued over $1 million in annual commitment value with 5 representing over $10 million in total contract value. Of these 5 deals, 2 were greater than $20 million, a new record this quarter." Notably, Elastic Security was chosen by one of the largest chemical manufacturers globally, and the company secured a $26 million commitment with CISA for a unified SIEM as-a-service offering.
  • Key product launches included Agent Builder, which "builds on the Elastic inference service and provides an out-of-the-box conversational experience, allowing users to interact directly with any data in Elastic search," and Streams, an "agentic AI solution that simplifies working with logs."
  • The acquisition of Jina AI was announced to expand multilingual and multimodal embedding and reranker models capabilities, with Kulkarni noting, "Jina AI extends and accelerates this strategy."
  • Navam Welihinda, CFO, stated, "We saw continued broad-based demand and notable strength in commitments across all geos, supported by healthy consumption trends."

Outlook

  • The company is raising its full fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance. For Q3 2026, total revenue is expected in the range of $437 million to $439 million, and sales-led subscription revenue in the range of $364 million to $366 million. Non-GAAP operating margin is expected to be approximately 17.5%.
  • For the full fiscal year 2026, Elastic expects total revenue in the range of $1.715 billion to $1.721 billion and sales-led subscription revenue in the range of $1.417 billion to $1.423 billion. Non-GAAP operating margin for the full year is projected at approximately 16.25%. Non-GAAP diluted EPS is expected to be in the range of $2.40 to $2.46.
  • Welihinda highlighted "we are raising our full fiscal year 2026 revenue guidance" and expects to use "more than 50% of our $500 million authorized amount in fiscal 2026" for the share repurchase program.

Financial Results

  • Total revenue in Q2 was $423 million, representing growth of 16% as reported. Sales-led subscription revenue reached $349 million, growing 18% as reported.
  • Current remaining performance obligation (CRPO) at the end of Q2 was approximately $971 million, growing 17% year-over-year. RPO grew 19% in the quarter.
  • Subscription gross margins reached 82%, total gross margins were 78%, and operating margin was 16.5%. Adjusted free cash flow was approximately $26 million, with a margin of 6%.
  • During Q2, Elastic returned approximately $114 million in cash to shareholders through share repurchases.

Q&A

  • Matthew Hedberg, RBC Capital Markets, asked about AI native and non-AI native customer consumption trends. Kulkarni responded, "we are seeing strong consumption across the board, even in our traditional businesses and not just in search, but also in observability and security."
  • Koji Ikeda, BofA Securities, inquired about sales-led subscription revenue composition and cloud growth expectations. Welihinda explained, "our goal is to drive what the sales team delivers from a commitment perspective, which is the self-managed commitments and the cloud commitments."
  • Sanjit Singh, Morgan Stanley, questioned sales productivity and AI monetization beyond RAG. Kulkarni noted, "Agent Builder will absolutely be yet another way for us to monetize our core strength in context engineering, but it's going to be an and."
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays, discussed slippage and guidance philosophy. Welihinda clarified, "the renewal side, obviously, is going to come in the year, and that's reflected by our strong year."
  • Additional analyst questions addressed deal composition, federal sector performance, and AI use case stickiness. Management consistently emphasized strong customer demand, healthy renewals, and robust pipeline.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts questioned the durability of sales-led growth, the impact of AI workloads, and the composition of large deals with a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on drivers of continued momentum and the implications for guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in both prepared statements and Q&A, frequently citing strong execution, record-setting large deals, and expanding pipeline. Kulkarni used phrases like "we feel really good about the stickiness of our usage" and "commitments have just been absolutely wonderful for us."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was even more confident, with heightened emphasis on large deal wins and AI-driven opportunities. Analysts maintained a similar level of cautious optimism as in Q1.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for both Q3 and FY26 was raised, with the revenue range increased from the prior quarter's outlook. Q2 revenue grew 16%, slightly lower than Q1's 20% reported growth.
  • Sales-led subscription revenue growth moderated to 18% from 22% in Q1, but management emphasized continued strong commitments and deal momentum.
  • Gross margins remained strong, while adjusted free cash flow margin declined in line with typical seasonality.
  • Management's confidence level increased, particularly regarding AI leadership and large deal momentum. Analysts’ focus shifted from price increases and model predictability in Q1 to deal composition, slippage, and the impact of AI in Q2.
  • Strategic focus evolved from broad-based product innovation and execution in Q1 to highlighting AI-driven large deals and new product launches in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged renewal slippage due to the U.S. government shutdown, with renewals expected to close in Q3. Welihinda stated, "we just expect those renewals to come in, in Q3. There wasn't anybody that was working during that shutdown in those agencies to be able to process those orders."
  • Seasonality and billing variability were mentioned as factors impacting quarterly results.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the composition of large deals, the pace of Gen AI adoption, and the concentration of growth in cloud versus self-managed segments.

Final Takeaway

Elastic delivered a robust Q2, highlighted by record-setting large deals, healthy AI-driven demand, and expanding product capabilities. The company raised its full-year guidance, supported by strong sales execution, continued customer expansion, and momentum in both cloud and self-managed offerings. Management remains confident in its long-term outlook, emphasizing the platform’s differentiation in AI, security, and observability as key drivers of future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Veeva signals continued CRM strength with 115 Vault CRM customers and robust Crossix growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV) Q3 2026

Management View

  • CEO Peter Gassner reported "an excellent Q3 with strength across the business and results above our guidance." He highlighted total revenue of $811 million and non-GAAP operating income of $365 million. Gassner emphasized, "Veeva AI is a major initiative for Veeva, and we're making excellent progress. We think Veeva AI can be significant for customers, the industry and Veeva." He also noted ongoing innovation "across all product areas, including Vault CRM, Crossix, Clinical and Safety."
  • Chief Financial Officer Brian Van Wagener stated, "CRM is about 20% of total revenue today, down from about 25% 2 years ago, and that's because other product areas have been growing." He explained that no material impact is expected from the migration of some customers away from Vault CRM for this year or next: "No impact expected this year and likely nothing material for next year either. Longer term, we don't expect any impact on our 2030 goals."
  • Executive Vice President of Strategy Paul Shawah described Crossix as in line with expectations and emphasized it "continues to be a nice growth driver, and we expect it to be that."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated confidence in its 2030 goals and said, "It's a diverse business, and that means there's a lot of paths to get there, and we're still on track."
  • Gassner signaled optimism about the clinical business: "Future is very bright in clinical."
  • On CRM, Van Wagener said, "We will also have the potential to win some of these customers back."
  • Paul Shawah stated, "Crossix continues to be a nice growth driver, and we expect it to be that. Although there may be some variability, we expect that to be a nice driver over the next several years."

Financial Results

  • Gassner reported "total revenue in the quarter was $811 million and non-GAAP operating income was $365 million."
  • Van Wagener pointed to a "modest headwind" in gross margins related to migration off Salesforce royalties, but called it "pretty immaterial in the grand scheme of things."
  • Over 115 customers are now live on Vault CRM, including top 20s. Van Wagener explained, "Gross margins on subscriptions were essentially stable, slightly up year-over-year."
  • Crossix was again called out as a pocket of strength, with Shawah saying, "You've seen nice outperformance of Crossix in the first couple of quarters of the year, and we expected that to continue to play out."

Q&A

  • Saket Kalia, Barclays: Questioned the revenue at risk from six top 20 customers not migrating to Vault CRM. Van Wagener responded that CRM is now a smaller part of revenue and no impact is expected this year or next.
  • Joseph Vruwink, Baird: Asked about the netting effect from retaining CRM customers and potential for add-ons. Van Wagener indicated potential for growth through add-ons and new innovations, and that the business remains healthy.
  • Brian Peterson, Raymond James: Inquired about AI's monetization opportunities. Gassner said opportunity is "broadly even across the board," especially in Safety and Clinical.
  • Alexei Gogolev, JPMorgan: Requested a deeper look at the pharma demand environment. Gassner replied, "The industry overall is pretty healthy."
  • Hoi-Fung Wong, Oppenheimer: Asked about Crossix performance. Shawah said, "Measurement business, very stable, and we've continued to perform well there."
  • Stanislav Berenshteyn, Wells Fargo: Asked about sales cycles for Safety and regulatory. Gassner indicated long cycles but increasing momentum, especially with AI in Safety.
  • Dylan Becker, William Blair: Explored the role of business consulting in platform adoption. Gassner emphasized, "We have to be an excellent consulting company. We have to be an excellent data company. We have to be an excellent software company."
  • Tyler Radke, Citi: Asked about unique CRM customer decisions and win-back timing. Gassner explained, "Usually, it comes with, honestly, executive turnover... or project failure, and then it can come in a hurry."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on CRM migration risks, revenue at risk, and competitive dynamics, reflecting a slightly skeptical to neutral sentiment. Questions frequently circled potential revenue loss and timing of customer wins and win-backs.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, emphasizing business diversity, innovation, and strong execution. Phrases such as "I feel good about the upside," "future is very bright," and "the business is good" underscored consistent confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains probing, but management’s confidence and composure are unchanged, echoing reassurance about long-term stability and growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language stayed consistent, with management reaffirming the 2030 targets and a diverse path to those goals.
  • CRM attrition and migration risks received more detailed attention, with explicit discussion of the top 20 customer base and win-back scenarios.
  • Crossix continued to be a strong growth area, with repeated mention of stable performance across both quarters.
  • Management’s confidence and strategic direction appear unchanged quarter-over-quarter, while analysts sustained their focus on CRM transitions and product adoption.

Risks and Concerns

  • Migration of six top 20 CRM customers to other solutions is acknowledged, but management sees limited financial risk in the near term and potential for future win-backs.
  • Long sales cycles are noted for Safety and regulatory products, adding an element of unpredictability to pipeline timing.
  • Analyst concerns persist regarding competitive threats in CRM and the risk that rivals could use recent wins as a foothold for future expansion.
  • Gassner described the risk as "customer-specific humans" and noted, "There's no particular pattern there."

Final Takeaway

Veeva Systems delivered a strong Q3, demonstrating robust growth and resilience across its diversified business lines. While CRM migration among top 20 customers remains in focus, management underscored that the business is increasingly driven by innovation and expansion in areas like Crossix, Clinical, and Safety, supported by ongoing investment in AI. The company remains confident in its long-term outlook and reiterated that recent CRM transitions are unlikely to materially impact its 2030 targets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Ross Stores raises Q4 comp sales forecast to 3%-4% amid robust branded strategy and holiday momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ross Stores, Inc. (ROST) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Conroy highlighted, "we are very pleased with our third quarter sales results, which accelerated from the prior quarter. Total sales for the period grew 10% to $5.6 billion, with comparable store sales increasing a strong 7%." He attributed the growth to compelling brand name assortments and new marketing campaigns, which led to "broad-based growth across all major merchandise categories." Conroy emphasized the successful back-to-school season and noted strong execution by the stores and supply chain teams.
  • Conroy reported, "Earnings per share for the 13 weeks ended November 1, 2025, were $1.58 on net income of $512 million," with a $0.05 per share negative impact from tariff-related costs. He stated, "we opened 36 new Ross and 4 dd's DISCOUNTS stores."
  • On strategy, Conroy explained the "branded strategy" has been fully embedded for over a year, particularly benefiting the ladies' business, which "further accelerated this quarter and comped above the chain average." The approach also strengthened vendor partnerships and increased closeout opportunities, helping to partially offset tariff impacts.
  • Executive VP & CFO William Sheehan stated, "comparable store sales rose 7% in the quarter. The gain was a result of both higher transactions and a larger average basket size." He added, "Operating margin decreased by 35 basis points to 11.6%, mainly due to the impact of tariffs." Sheehan confirmed $262 million in share repurchases during the quarter and maintained the plan to repurchase $1.05 billion in shares for the year.

Outlook

  • Sheehan announced, "for the 13 weeks ending January 31, 2026, we are raising our comparable store sales forecast to be up 3% to 4% with earnings per share in the range of $1.77 to $1.85." He explained this guidance reflects approximately $0.03 per share in unfavorable timing of packaway-related expenses that benefited Q3.
  • Fiscal 2025 earnings per share guidance was updated to $6.38 to $6.46, with Sheehan noting, "we now forecast the fourth quarter impact [of tariffs] to be negligible, leading to a full year cost of approximately $0.16 per share."
  • Sheehan also said, "Total sales are projected to increase 6% to 8%. We expect operating margin to be in the range of 11.5% to 11.8% compared to 12.4% last year."

Financial Results

  • Total sales in Q3 rose 10% to $5.6 billion, with comparable store sales up 7%.
  • Operating margin for the quarter was 11.6%, impacted by tariffs and increased distribution costs, but offset by lower domestic freight and occupancy costs.
  • EPS for Q3 was $1.58, including a $0.05 per share negative tariff impact.
  • Year-to-date sales reached $16.1 billion, with comparable sales up 3%.
  • Inventories increased 9% year-over-year, with average store inventories up 15% as inventory build for the holiday season was advanced.

Q&A

  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan: Asked about the drivers of the 500 basis point acceleration in same-store sales. Conroy responded, "the merchandise categories every single merchandise category in the third quarter, every single major merchandise category anyway was positive or nicely positive... We also had broad-based strength across the country in terms of our geographic regions."
  • Corey Tarlowe, Jefferies: Inquired about the stickiest changes propelling multiyear growth. Conroy said the strategy is to get "merchandising, marketing and stores, perhaps add supply chain to that mix, operating in unison."
  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about tariff mitigation and AUR trends. Hartshorn explained, "our merchant teams have done a tremendous job balancing cost concessions with modest market-driven price increases... given the closeout availability, take advantage of closeouts in the marketplace and chase above-plan sales."
  • Charles Grom, Gordon Haskett: Questioned if marketing spend would increase. Conroy responded, "Right now, we're going to maintain our percent of sales where it is."
  • Lorraine Maikis, BofA: Probed the opportunity in the ladies' business. Conroy indicated, "the ladies business was actually comp enhancing."
  • Additional questions covered store refreshes, value gap strategy, self-checkout, dd's DISCOUNTS performance, inventory build, demographic trends, and the potential for loyalty programs.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts had a slightly positive tone, focusing on the sustainability of recent growth, the impact of strategic changes, and asking for more detail on forward momentum and risk mitigation.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, especially in prepared remarks, with Conroy emphasizing broad-based improvements and Sheehan raising guidance. During Q&A, management was open about early innings for some initiatives and cautious about overcommitting to higher expenses.
  • Compared to last quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with more assertive forward-looking guidance and recognition of improved operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism and a 2%-3% comp guide in Q2 to a more assertive 3%-4% range for Q4.
  • Strategic focus advanced from embedding the branded strategy to achieving broad-based category and regional growth and driving improvements in the ladies' business.
  • Analysts' attention moved from sequential improvement and tariff mitigation to the stickiness of performance drivers and the scalability of recent successes.
  • Key metrics such as sales growth and operating margins improved, while management tone shifted toward greater confidence, supported by stronger results and higher guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Tariff-related costs, though expected to be negligible in Q4, remain a risk as management noted, "tariff uncertainties persist."
  • Distribution cost increases were cited, primarily from the opening of a new distribution center and processing costs.
  • Management addressed inventory build as a holiday season risk, expressing confidence in inventory health and positioning.
  • Analysts questioned sustainability of the comp acceleration and the ability to continue leveraging branded strategies and marketing without materially increasing spend.

Final Takeaway

Ross Stores management reported strong Q3 results with double-digit sales growth and robust comparable sales, driven by effective merchandising, marketing initiatives, and new store openings. The company raised its Q4 comp sales forecast to 3%-4% and increased full-year EPS guidance, citing broad-based category strength and successful mitigation of tariff impacts. Leadership highlighted ongoing opportunities in branding, store refreshes, and supply chain enhancements, while remaining focused on operating discipline and value for customers as they enter the holiday season.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Intuit reaffirms $21B revenue outlook and highlights 29% payment volume growth amid AI-driven platform expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Intuit Inc. (INTU) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Sasan Goodarzi, CEO, highlighted that "we delivered an outstanding quarter with Q1 revenue growth of 18%, reflecting the exceptional momentum we have across the company." The company is intensifying its focus on AI-driven expert platform strategy, with specific emphasis on delivering done-for-you experiences via AI and human intelligence (HI), accelerating money movement, and strengthening partnerships with accounting firms for mid-market expansion.
  • Goodarzi announced the unveiling of Intuit Intelligence, an AI-native system of intelligence currently in beta with thousands of customers, and the launch of Intuit Accountant Suite, an offering designed to transform accounting firm efficiency. The CEO reported, "we're continuing to see momentum with our virtual team of AI agent with 2.8 million customers leveraging these agents to do the work for them."
  • The CEO emphasized significant growth in virtual agent adoption, noting QuickBooks Live customer growth of 61% in Q1 and total online payment volume growth for payments and bill pay customers of 29%. Mid-market online ecosystem revenue for QBO Advanced and Intuit Enterprise Suite grew approximately 40%.
  • Goodarzi reported on expanding partnerships with top accounting firms and growing contract wins, stating "the total number of IES contracts at the end of the quarter was nearly 50% higher than it was at the end of Q4."
  • Consumer platform highlights included TurboTax Live revenue growth of 51% and continued strong performance from Credit Karma.
  • Sandeep Aujla, CFO, stated, "we delivered a strong first quarter of fiscal 2026 across the company. Our first quarter results include revenue of $3.9 billion, up 18%; GAAP operating income of $534 million versus $271 million last year. Non-GAAP operating income of $1.3 billion versus $953 million last year; GAAP diluted earnings per share of $1.59 versus $0.70 a year ago and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $3.34 versus $2.50 last year."
  • CFO Aujla also announced the departure of Kim Watkins as Vice President of Investor Relations, with Geoff Koegler stepping in as acting Head of IR.

Outlook

  • Intuit reaffirmed its fiscal 2026 guidance, expecting total company revenue of $20.997 billion to $21.186 billion, representing 12% to 13% growth. Guidance includes Global Business Solutions Group revenue growth of 14% to 15%, and Consumer Group revenue growth of 8% to 9%, with TurboTax growth of 8%, Credit Karma growth of 10% to 13%, and ProTax growth of 2% to 3%.
  • GAAP diluted earnings per share are guided to $15.49 to $15.69, and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share of $22.98 to $23.18.
  • Q2 guidance includes total company revenue growth of 14% to 15%, GAAP EPS of $1.76 to $1.81, and non-GAAP EPS of $3.63 to $3.68.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 was $3.9 billion, up 18% from the prior year. GAAP operating income was $534 million, and non-GAAP operating income reached $1.3 billion. GAAP diluted EPS was $1.59, and non-GAAP diluted EPS was $3.34.
  • Global Business Solutions Group revenue grew 18% (20% excluding Mailchimp), with online ecosystem revenue up 21% (25% excluding Mailchimp). QuickBooks Online Accounting revenue increased 25%.
  • Consumer platform Q1 revenue grew 21%, with Credit Karma revenue up 27%, TurboTax revenue up 6%, and ProTax revenue up 15%.
  • The company ended the quarter with $3.7 billion in cash and investments and $6.1 billion in debt. It repurchased $851 million of stock and will pay a quarterly dividend of $1.20 per share on January 16, 2026.

Q&A

  • S. Kirk Materne, Evercore ISI: Asked about the OpenAI partnership and its impact on revenue share and data privacy. CEO Goodarzi responded that "there is no revenue share. And so the economics that we enjoy today when we directly work with customers, we will enjoy tomorrow," emphasizing unchanged data privacy and security.
  • Sitikantha Panigrahi, Mizuho Securities: Inquired about mid-market AI impact and expansion plans. Goodarzi outlined increasing awareness, platform innovation, and early-stage accountant partnerships, with productivity improvements and potential headcount additions in the coming quarters.
  • Brad Zelnick, Deutsche Bank: Questioned TurboTax innovations and local office expansion. Goodarzi expressed high optimism for the upcoming tax season, citing 300 experiments and expanded local presence. CFO Aujla noted, "customers convert 5x better when there's a local expert within a 50-mile radius."
  • Keith Weiss, Morgan Stanley: Asked about U.S. consumer health and business durability. Goodarzi observed "profits and cash flows are stable and up" and noted industry-specific trends, while Aujla highlighted Intuit's products as "must-have" even in tougher economies.
  • Mark Murphy, JPMorgan: Sought clarity on Credit Karma’s market share gains. Goodarzi described the integration of TurboTax and Credit Karma, emphasizing ongoing runway and early stages of platform potential.
  • Daniel Jester, BMO: Asked about Mailchimp growth drivers. Goodarzi said double-digit growth will require both mid-market and small customer improvements, with momentum expected to be clearer in early spring.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts demonstrated a positive tone, frequently offering congratulations and focusing on innovation, growth, and strategic initiatives. Concerns centered on the durability of momentum, macroeconomic implications, and Mailchimp’s growth trajectory, but questions were generally constructive.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and optimistic, especially on AI-driven strategies, platform expansion, and consumer engagement. Goodarzi frequently used phrases like "we are well positioned" and "we are excited," reflecting high confidence in sustained growth. During Q&A, management remained upbeat and provided detailed responses, showing little defensiveness or hesitation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management tones remained positive, with current sentiment reflecting increased optimism due to higher growth metrics and successful AI platform adoption.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained its fiscal 2026 guidance from the previous quarter, signaling consistent confidence in growth targets.
  • Q1 revenue growth of 18% accelerated from prior trends, with notable increases in QuickBooks Live, TurboTax Live, and Credit Karma revenues.
  • Management highlighted greater adoption of AI-driven features and expanded partnerships, compared to a focus on initial launches and early engagement in the prior quarter.
  • Analysts continued to probe Mailchimp growth and AI monetization, but current questions reflected heightened interest in concrete AI-driven results and platform-level integration.
  • Management’s tone sustained its optimism, but with added emphasis on tangible outcomes from AI investments and higher customer engagement metrics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing challenges with Mailchimp, indicating progress in mid-market but noting the need for improvements among smaller customers to reach double-digit growth.
  • The company addressed industry-specific volatility, particularly in real estate and lending, but reported overall stability in profits and cash flows across its customer base.
  • Analyst questions highlighted concerns about macroeconomic headwinds, the competitive landscape, and timing for Mailchimp’s revenue acceleration.

Final Takeaway

Intuit’s first quarter of fiscal 2026 showcased robust revenue and earnings growth, powered by rapid adoption of AI-driven solutions and a strengthening platform strategy. Management reaffirmed full-year guidance and expressed high confidence in the ability to drive durable growth across both business and consumer segments. Strategic partnerships, product innovation, and the expansion of AI and human intelligence capabilities are expected to continue fueling momentum, with management projecting a strong outlook for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil extends losses on renewed U.S. peace efforts for Russia-Ukraine
data.description:
Crude oil futures fell Thursday, adding to the previous session's 2% decline, as investors weighed risks to Russian supplies from the Trump administration's latest plan to restart peace talks between Russia and Ukraine, ahead of the Friday deadline for companies to cut their dealings with the two Russian oil majors.

President Trump sent a Pentagon delegation to Kyiv in a bid to revive negotiations aimed at ending the war in Ukraine, which could ultimately ease supply disruption concerns as a resolution could lead to the lifting of sanctions on Russian crude.

The U.S.-Russia peace proposal includes concessions of Ukrainian territory to Russia and reductions in Ukraine's armed forces, both of which Ukraine's President Zelenskiy has previously rejected, although he said he would study the new plan.

"If a ceasefire in Ukraine does come to fruition and sanctions on Russia are lifted, I would think WTI crude oil trades sharply lower and takes out the $55.12 level from April 9," Mizuho analyst Robert Yawger said in a note.

The U.S. sanctions on Russia's major oil producers that come into effect Friday have been supporting oil prices and countering market concerns about coming oversupply.

The sanctions could leave nearly 48 million barrels of Russian crude stranded on the water, pushing dozens of tankers to scramble for alternative destinations, Bloomberg reported.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for December delivery closed -0.5% to $59.14/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for January delivery ended -0.2% to $63.38/bbl, the lowest settlement price for both benchmarks in a week.

Nymex diesel (HO1:COM) fell for a second session, as the Nymex December front-month contract finished -3.9% to $2.533/gal after a nearly $0.15/gal spurt during a three-day rally.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) finished lower as the U.S. Energy Information Administration reported a 14 Bcf inventory draw for last week, the first withdrawal of the season and in line with expectations; the front-month Nymex December contract settled -1.7% at $4.474/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)
